APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): This paper is squarely within the scope of a top NLP venue: it presents a new Singlish UD treebank (1,200 sentences) and tackles dependency parsing and POS tagging for a low-resource dialect using cross-lingual transfer. The methods and evaluations match community standards (bi-LSTM-CRF POS tagger, Dozat & Manning biaffine parser, UAS/LAS metrics) and it compares sensible baselines including English-on-Singlish and ICE-SIN embeddings. Making the annotation and parser/code available further positions the work as a useful resource and method contribution for the NLP research community. | B(5): This paper is squarely within the scope of a top NLP venue: it builds a new Singlish UD treebank (1,200 sentences) for an under-resourced language variety and demonstrates relevant cross-lingual transfer methods (feature-level neural stacking) using standard neural models (bi-LSTM-CRF POS tagger, Dozat & Manning biaffine parser). Evaluation uses community-standard metrics (UAS/LAS, POS accuracy) and comparisons to reasonable baselines (Singlish-only, ENG-on-SIN, ICE-SIN), and the authors release code and data to enable follow-up work. Given the relevance to dependency parsing, low-resource transfer, and resource creation under the UD framework, the paper is a strong fit for a top-tier NLP/ML conference.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper is squarely within the scope of a top NLP venue: it presents a new Singlish UD treebank (1,200 sentences) and tackles dependency parsing and POS tagging for a low-resource dialect using cross-lingual transfer. The methods and evaluations match community standards (bi-LSTM-CRF POS tagger, Dozat & Manning biaffine parser, UAS/LAS metrics) and it compares sensible baselines including English-on-Singlish and ICE-SIN embeddings. Making the annotation and parser/code available further positions the work as a useful resource and method contribution for the NLP research community.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is squarely within the scope of a top NLP venue: it builds a new Singlish UD treebank (1,200 sentences) for an under-resourced language variety and demonstrates relevant cross-lingual transfer methods (feature-level neural stacking) using standard neural models (bi-LSTM-CRF POS tagger, Dozat & Manning biaffine parser). Evaluation uses community-standard metrics (UAS/LAS, POS accuracy) and comparisons to reasonable baselines (Singlish-only, ENG-on-SIN, ICE-SIN), and the authors release code and data to enable follow-up work. Given the relevance to dependency parsing, low-resource transfer, and resource creation under the UD framework, the paper is a strong fit for a top-tier NLP/ML conference.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The experiments are solid and generally comprehensive: the authors build a 1,200-sentence Singlish UD treebank, train POS and dependency models, compare a Singlish-only baseline, ENG-on-SIN transfer, and neural stacking, report POS accuracy and UAS/LAS (including a 36.51% relative error reduction) and study different embeddings (ICE-SIN, Giga100M). Ablation/sensitivity is partially addressed via baseline vs stacked comparisons and embedding variants, but there is no explicit component-wise ablation or deeper sensitivity analysis of the stacking architecture reported in the summary. Related work coverage is said to be thorough, limitations and failure cases (small Singlish training data, NP/copy deletion handling) are discussed, and code plus hyperparameters are provided for reproducibility. | B(4): The experiments are solid and generally comprehensive: the authors build a 1,200-sentence Singlish UD treebank, compare Singlish-only and English-transfer baselines (ENG-on-SIN, Base-ICE-SIN), evaluate POS and dependency metrics (POS accuracy, UAS, LAS) and show neural stacking yields large gains (e.g., 84.21% UAS, 36.51% relative error reduction). They include sensible ablations/sensitivity analyses — testing ICE-SIN vs Giga embeddings, neural stacking vs base models, and per-phenomenon analyses (e.g., Table 4 and Figure 5) — and report limitations (small Singlish data, NP/copy deletion handling). Reproducibility is supported (code and hyperparameters provided) and related work coverage is stated as thorough, so the work merits a high but not perfect score due to the remaining data-size constraints and a few minor performance gaps noted.

reviewer_a.justification: The experiments are solid and generally comprehensive: the authors build a 1,200-sentence Singlish UD treebank, train POS and dependency models, compare a Singlish-only baseline, ENG-on-SIN transfer, and neural stacking, report POS accuracy and UAS/LAS (including a 36.51% relative error reduction) and study different embeddings (ICE-SIN, Giga100M). Ablation/sensitivity is partially addressed via baseline vs stacked comparisons and embedding variants, but there is no explicit component-wise ablation or deeper sensitivity analysis of the stacking architecture reported in the summary. Related work coverage is said to be thorough, limitations and failure cases (small Singlish training data, NP/copy deletion handling) are discussed, and code plus hyperparameters are provided for reproducibility.

reviewer_b.justification: The experiments are solid and generally comprehensive: the authors build a 1,200-sentence Singlish UD treebank, compare Singlish-only and English-transfer baselines (ENG-on-SIN, Base-ICE-SIN), evaluate POS and dependency metrics (POS accuracy, UAS, LAS) and show neural stacking yields large gains (e.g., 84.21% UAS, 36.51% relative error reduction). They include sensible ablations/sensitivity analyses — testing ICE-SIN vs Giga embeddings, neural stacking vs base models, and per-phenomenon analyses (e.g., Table 4 and Figure 5) — and report limitations (small Singlish data, NP/copy deletion handling). Reproducibility is supported (code and hyperparameters provided) and related work coverage is stated as thorough, so the work merits a high but not perfect score due to the remaining data-size constraints and a few minor performance gaps noted.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims are supported by experimental evidence: they built a 1,200-sentence Singlish UD treebank, trained standard bi-LSTM-CRF and biaffine parsers, and report concrete gains from feature-level neural stacking (POS 84.79% and parser UAS 84.21% with a reported 36.51% relative error reduction). The methodology appears sound with appropriate baselines (Singlish-only, ENG-on-SIN, ICE-SIN embeddings) and use of relevant datasets, and model/hyperparameter details are reported. Minor technical concerns remain: no mention of statistical significance testing of improvements and the small treebank size (1,200 sentences) limits claims about generality; also some degradation on specific phenomena (NP deletion) is acknowledged. | B(4): The main claims (creation of a 1,200-sentence Singlish UD treebank; training of POS tagger and dependency parser; and improvement via feature-level neural stacking yielding 84.79% POS accuracy and 84.21% UAS with a reported 36.51% relative error reduction) are backed by experiments comparing sensible baselines (Singlish-only, ENG-on-SIN, and embedding variants) with hyperparameters reported and code released. There are no mathematical derivations to check, and the methodology (bi-LSTM-CRF tagger, biaffine parser, neural stacking, use of UD-Eng and ICE-SIN data) is technically sound. Minor concerns: the Singlish training set is small (1,200 sentences) and no statistical significance testing is mentioned, which limits confidence in generalization though it does not invalidate the reported improvements. Overall the results and conclusions are justified by the presented experimental evidence aside from these minor issues.

reviewer_a.justification: The main claims are supported by experimental evidence: they built a 1,200-sentence Singlish UD treebank, trained standard bi-LSTM-CRF and biaffine parsers, and report concrete gains from feature-level neural stacking (POS 84.79% and parser UAS 84.21% with a reported 36.51% relative error reduction). The methodology appears sound with appropriate baselines (Singlish-only, ENG-on-SIN, ICE-SIN embeddings) and use of relevant datasets, and model/hyperparameter details are reported. Minor technical concerns remain: no mention of statistical significance testing of improvements and the small treebank size (1,200 sentences) limits claims about generality; also some degradation on specific phenomena (NP deletion) is acknowledged.

reviewer_b.justification: The main claims (creation of a 1,200-sentence Singlish UD treebank; training of POS tagger and dependency parser; and improvement via feature-level neural stacking yielding 84.79% POS accuracy and 84.21% UAS with a reported 36.51% relative error reduction) are backed by experiments comparing sensible baselines (Singlish-only, ENG-on-SIN, and embedding variants) with hyperparameters reported and code released. There are no mathematical derivations to check, and the methodology (bi-LSTM-CRF tagger, biaffine parser, neural stacking, use of UD-Eng and ICE-SIN data) is technically sound. Minor concerns: the Singlish training set is small (1,200 sentences) and no statistical significance testing is mentioned, which limits confidence in generalization though it does not invalidate the reported improvements. Overall the results and conclusions are justified by the presented experimental evidence aside from these minor issues.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper contributes a clearly new resource — a Singlish UD treebank of 1,200 sentences — and applies existing state-of-the-art models (bi-LSTM-CRF tagger, Dozat & Manning parser) plus feature-level neural stacking to transfer English knowledge. However, the core techniques (neural stacking from Chen et al., 2016 and biaffine parsing) are not novel; the work is primarily an application and empirical evaluation of known methods to a low-resource Singlish setting. The combination and the resource are useful and moderately novel, but do not introduce fundamentally new methods. | B(3): The main novelty is empirical and resource-oriented: creation of a 1,200-sentence Singlish UD treebank and an evaluation showing transfer from English yields substantial gains. Methodologically the paper primarily applies existing techniques (biaffine parser of Dozat & Manning, neural stacking of Chen et al.) and embeddings (ICE-SIN, Giga100M) rather than introducing new algorithms. The contribution is a valuable, domain-specific application and dataset rather than a fundamentally new method, so it represents a moderate (incremental but useful) novelty to the field.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper contributes a clearly new resource — a Singlish UD treebank of 1,200 sentences — and applies existing state-of-the-art models (bi-LSTM-CRF tagger, Dozat & Manning parser) plus feature-level neural stacking to transfer English knowledge. However, the core techniques (neural stacking from Chen et al., 2016 and biaffine parsing) are not novel; the work is primarily an application and empirical evaluation of known methods to a low-resource Singlish setting. The combination and the resource are useful and moderately novel, but do not introduce fundamentally new methods.

reviewer_b.justification: The main novelty is empirical and resource-oriented: creation of a 1,200-sentence Singlish UD treebank and an evaluation showing transfer from English yields substantial gains. The contribution is a valuable, domain-specific application and dataset rather than a fundamentally new method, so it represents a moderate (incremental but useful) novelty to the field.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Creating a Singlish UD treebank (1,200 sentences) and releasing a POS tagger and dependency parser addresses an important gap for an under-resourced World English variety and provides a concrete evaluation benchmark and tools. The demonstrated 36.51% relative error reduction via feature-level neural stacking (best UAS 84.21%, POS 84.79%) shows transfer from English can substantially improve performance on non-standard varieties, which will influence how researchers approach cross-lingual/variety transfer. Making annotations and code available increases likelihood of reuse and follow-up work. The limited training size and some remaining failure cases (e.g., NP deletion) constrain immediate broad adoption but do not negate the paper’s clear value for opening new research directions. | B(4): Creating and releasing a Singlish UD treebank (1,200 sentences) and associated parser code fills an important low-resource gap for a major English variety, making a tangible resource contribution. The empirical results—neural stacking yielding a best UAS of 84.21% and a 36.51% relative error reduction—demonstrate an effective and practical cross-lingual transfer method for non-standard varieties. The observation that existing UD relation definitions can accommodate Singlish deviations and the availability of models/code make the work likely to be built upon and to open up follow-up research on transfer to other creoles/varieties.

reviewer_a.justification: Creating a Singlish UD treebank (1,200 sentences) and releasing a POS tagger and dependency parser addresses an important gap for an under-resourced World English variety and provides a concrete evaluation benchmark and tools. The demonstrated 36.51% relative error reduction via feature-level neural stacking (best UAS 84.21%, POS 84.79%) shows transfer from English can substantially improve performance on non-standard varieties, which will influence how researchers approach cross-lingual/variety transfer. Making annotations and code available increases likelihood of reuse and follow-up work. The limited training size and some remaining failure cases (e.g., NP deletion) constrain immediate broad adoption but do not negate the paper’s clear value for opening new research directions.

The observation that existing UD relation definitions can accommodate Singlish deviations and the availability of models/code make the work likely to be built upon and to open up follow-up research on transfer to other creoles/varieties.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Based on the summary, the paper is well-organized (methods, experiments, conclusions presented coherently) and clearly states its main contributions early (creation of a Singlish UD treebank, POS tagger/parser, and transfer via neural stacking). Notation and experimental reporting appear consistent and well-documented (metrics like UAS/LAS/POS accuracy, hyperparameters, datasets and baselines are explicitly reported), and results are presented with concrete numbers that aid clarity. Writing quality is described as good and related work coverage thorough, suggesting only minor presentation issues rather than major clarity problems. | B(4): The paper is well-organized (datasets, methods, experiments, conclusions) and states its main contributions clearly early (creation of a 1,200-sentence Singlish UD treebank, POS tagger and parser with neural stacking). Notation and experimental reporting appear consistent and complete (UAS/LAS/POS metrics, hyperparameters reported, code released), and the presentation of results (comparisons to baselines, reported error reductions) is informative. Writing quality is good overall, with only minor presentation issues implied by a few noted limitations rather than clarity problems.

reviewer_a.justification: Based on the summary, the paper is well-organized (methods, experiments, conclusions presented coherently) and clearly states its main contributions early (creation of a Singlish UD treebank, POS tagger/parser, and transfer via neural stacking). Notation and experimental reporting appear consistent and well-documented (metrics like UAS/LAS/POS accuracy, hyperparameters, datasets and baselines are explicitly reported), and results are presented with concrete numbers that aid clarity. Writing quality is described as good and related work coverage thorough, suggesting only minor presentation issues rather than major clarity problems.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well-organized (datasets, methods, experiments, conclusions) and states its main contributions clearly early (creation of a 1,200-sentence Singlish UD treebank, POS tagger and parser with neural stacking). Notation and experimental reporting appear consistent and complete (UAS/LAS/POS metrics, hyperparameters reported, code released), and the presentation of results (comparisons to baselines, reported error reductions) is informative. MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(5): The paper compares against appropriate, recent baselines (a Singlish-trained Dozat & Manning 2016 base parser, an English parser applied to Singlish, and Singlish models using ICE‑SIN embeddings) and reports standard metrics (UAS/LAS/POS accuracy) on the same Singlish treebank. Comparisons appear fair and conditions consistent, with caveats noted (e.g., differing fine-grained POS usage affecting English LAS). The authors acknowledge cases where prior methods perform comparably (neural stacking slightly worse on NP deletion) and analyze differences (relative error reduction, data sparsity effects), and they build on and cite relevant competing approaches (Dozat & Manning; Chen et al. neural stacking). | B(4): The paper compares against strong and relevant baselines: a Singlish-only base parser (Dozat & Manning 2016), an English parser applied to Singlish (ENG-on-SIN), and Singlish models using ICE-SIN embeddings, and reports standard metrics (UAS/LAS, POS accuracy) on the same Singlish treebank. Comparisons appear fair and conditions consistent, the authors analyze where neural stacking helps and acknowledge cases (e.g., NP deletion) where prior methods perform comparably or better. They also cite and build on relevant prior work (Dozat & Manning; Chen et al.) and report relative error reduction. Overall the evaluation is good, with only minor omissions of other potential modern transfer baselines not evident in the summary.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against appropriate, recent baselines (a Singlish-trained Dozat & Manning 2016 base parser, an English parser applied to Singlish, and Singlish models using ICE‑SIN embeddings) and reports standard metrics (UAS/LAS/POS accuracy) on the same Singlish treebank. Comparisons appear fair and conditions consistent, with caveats noted (e.g., differing fine-grained POS usage affecting English LAS). The authors acknowledge cases where prior methods perform comparably (neural stacking slightly worse on NP deletion) and analyze differences (relative error reduction, data sparsity effects), and they build on and cite relevant competing approaches (Dozat & Manning; Chen et al. neural stacking).

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against strong and relevant baselines: a Singlish-only base parser (Dozat & Manning 2016), an English parser applied to Singlish (ENG-on-SIN), and Singlish models using ICE-SIN embeddings, and reports standard metrics (UAS/LAS, POS accuracy) on the same Singlish treebank. Comparisons appear fair and conditions consistent, the authors analyze where neural stacking helps and acknowledge cases (e.g., NP deletion) where prior methods perform comparably or better. They also cite and build on relevant prior work (Dozat & Manning; Chen et al.) and report relative error reduction. Overall the evaluation is good, with only minor omissions of other potential modern transfer baselines not evident in the summary.

main_claims: We created a Singlish dependency treebank of 1,200 sentences under the Universal Dependencies scheme.
A Singlish POS tagger and dependency parser were trained and improved by transferring English knowledge using feature-level neural stacking.

Neural stacking yields substantial improvements: the stacked POS tagger achieves 84.79% accuracy and the best stacked parser (with ICE-SIN embeddings) achieves a UAS of 84.21%, corresponding to 36.51% relative error reduction over the baseline.
Existing English dependency relation definitions can accommodate Singlish lexical and grammatical deviations, and both the annotation and the parser are made available for research.
_summary.methodology: Construct a Singlish UD treebank and train neural models: a bi-LSTM-CRF POS tagger and a biaffine-attention graph-based dependency parser (Dozat & Manning, 2016), then apply feature-level neural stacking (Chen et al., 2016) to integrate pre-trained English models and embeddings into the Singlish models.
datasets: ICE-SIN (Singapore component of the International Corpus of English)
English Gigaword (used to sample data for Giga100M embeddings)
baselines: Base Singlish parser (Dozat & Manning, 2016 base model trained on Singlish)
English parser trained on UD-Eng applied to Singlish (ENG-on-SIN)
Singlish-only POS tagger and parser using ICE-SIN embeddings (Base-ICE-SIN)
limitations: Limited amount of Singlish training data caused poor POS tagging performance when trained on Singlish alone.
The neural stacking model has slightly lower performance on NP Deletion cases compared to some baselines.
The authors did not use fine-grained POS tags for the English parser, contributing to differences in reported English LAS.
Deleted copula and deleted noun-phrases in Singlish are not recovered in the annotations (they are ignored).
_summary.topic_area: NLP - dependency parsing / cross-lingual transfer for Singlish
